title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption: In 2024, per capita CO2 emissions and per capita fossil fuel energy consumption show a strong positive relationship across countries, plotted on logarithmic scales. The horizontal axis measures average energy from coal, oil, and gas in kilowatt-hours per person, while the vertical axis measures emissions in tonnes per person, including fossil fuel and industry sources but excluding land-use change. High-income countries, marked in dark green, cluster in the upper right quadrant, with the United States, South Korea, and Singapore positioned above 10 tonnes of emissions and 50,000 kWh of energy consumption. Qatar and Kuwait appear as outliers at the extreme top right, exceeding 20 tonnes of emissions. In contrast, lower-middle-income countries like Pakistan and the Philippines occupy the bottom left, with values below 1 tonne of emissions and 5,000 kWh of energy. Circle sizes correspond to population, making India and China visually prominent in the middle of the distribution. The data points form a tight diagonal band, indicating that higher fossil fuel energy use per person aligns with higher carbon emissions per person.
